items.passage: Coral reefs are among the most crowded habitats found anywhere in the ocean. They grow slowly as tiny animals called polyps build hard skeletons from minerals in the water. These structures shelter thousands of fish that depend on the reef for food. Warmer seas can force corals to expel the colorful algae living inside them. Scientists monitor reefs closely because their decline threatens countless species across tropical coastal zones.

Rising global temperatures are reshaping weather patterns across every continent on the planet. Greenhouse gases trap heat that would otherwise escape into space, warming the lower atmosphere. This process causes glaciers to melt and sea levels to climb along vulnerable coasts. Farmers face longer droughts and stronger storms that damage crops during the growing season. Researchers track these changes carefully to predict how ecosystems might adapt in coming decades.

Sleep plays a vital role in helping the human brain consolidate its recent memories. During the deepest stages, the mind replays experiences from the day and strengthens them. People who sleep poorly often struggle to concentrate and to reason clearly the next morning. Dreams tend to emerge during a distinct phase marked by rapid movement beneath the eyelids. Researchers report that consistent sleep routines improve both the mood and the memory of adults.

The mechanical clock transformed how people organized their daily lives in medieval Europe. Before its invention, towns had relied on the sun and church bells to mark the hours. Skilled craftsmen built towering devices whose turning gears divided each day into equal segments. These machines let markets, schools, and traders follow one shared and dependable daily schedule. Historians regard the clock as among the most influential inventions of that entire period.

Mountains rise slowly when powerful forces deep inside the earth push against solid rock. Where two plates collide, their edges buckle and fold upward into towering ranges. This process spans millions of years and continues even where peaks appear finished today. Wind, ice, and flowing rivers gradually carve the slopes into sharp ridges and valleys. Geologists study these features to understand how landscapes change across enormous stretches of time.

The asteroid belt is a broad region of rocky bodies orbiting between Mars and Jupiter. Millions of these fragments vary in size from tiny grains to enormous boulders miles wide. The powerful gravity of nearby Jupiter prevented them from merging into a single stable planet. Occasionally two of the objects collide and scatter smaller pieces onto entirely fresh orbits. Astronomers examine this belt for clues about how the early solar system originally developed.
